title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock

frames.caption: Start with minPrice = int.MaxValue (no buy yet) and best = 0. Sweep right (today's sell day) across prices once.
frames.note: This is the "degenerate" window: `minPrice` is the cheapest buy seen so far (the left edge) and `right` is today. There is no shrink loop — left just snaps down whenever a cheaper price appears, and we record the best gap as right sweeps forward.

frames.caption: right=0: today's price prices[0] = 7. Is it a new low (< minPrice int.MaxValue)?
frames.note: Each day is one of two things: a cheaper BUY (lower the floor) or a SELL candidate against the cheapest buy so far. The single comparison prices[right] < minPrice decides which.

frames.caption: 7 < old min, so minPrice = 7. The buy point snaps to index 0 — this is now the cheapest day to buy. best stays 0.
frames.note: Today is the cheapest day so far, so it becomes the new BUY point — the window's left edge snaps down to it. We never sell on the day we just lowered the floor (you can't sell below what you paid the same instant), so this branch only moves the buy point; profit is read on later days. `minPrice` only ever decreases — it is the running minimum, the one piece of state this whole window needs.

frames.caption: right=1: today's price prices[1] = 1. Is it a new low (< minPrice 7)?
frames.note: Each day is one of two things: a cheaper BUY (lower the floor) or a SELL candidate against the cheapest buy so far. The single comparison prices[right] < minPrice decides which.

frames.caption: 1 < old min, so minPrice = 1. The buy point snaps to index 1 — this is now the cheapest day to buy. best stays 0.
frames.note: Today is the cheapest day so far, so it becomes the new BUY point — the window's left edge snaps down to it. We never sell on the day we just lowered the floor (you can't sell below what you paid the same instant), so this branch only moves the buy point; profit is read on later days. `minPrice` only ever decreases — it is the running minimum, the one piece of state this whole window needs.

frames.caption: right=2: today's price prices[2] = 5. Is it a new low (< minPrice 1)?
frames.note: Each day is one of two things: a cheaper BUY (lower the floor) or a SELL candidate against the cheapest buy so far. The single comparison prices[right] < minPrice decides which.

frames.caption: Sell today: 5 − minPrice 1 = 4. best = max(0, 4) = 4. (buy@1 → sell@5)
frames.note: Today is NOT a new low, so it is a candidate SELL day. The best you could do is sell now against the cheapest buy seen so far: prices[right] − minPrice. Keep the running maximum in `best`. This is the entire trick — "min-so-far + running best" — a one-directional window where left snaps to each new minimum and right sweeps forward, never a shrink loop. Because each price is read exactly once, the scan is O(n) time and O(1) space.

frames.caption: right=3: today's price prices[3] = 3. Is it a new low (< minPrice 1)?
frames.note: Each day is one of two things: a cheaper BUY (lower the floor) or a SELL candidate against the cheapest buy so far. The single comparison prices[right] < minPrice decides which.

frames.caption: Sell today: 3 − minPrice 1 = 2. best = max(4, 2) = 4. (buy@1 → sell@3)
frames.note: Today is NOT a new low, so it is a candidate SELL day. The best you could do is sell now against the cheapest buy seen so far: prices[right] − minPrice. Keep the running maximum in `best`. This is the entire trick — "min-so-far + running best" — a one-directional window where left snaps to each new minimum and right sweeps forward, never a shrink loop. Because each price is read exactly once, the scan is O(n) time and O(1) space.

frames.caption: right=4: today's price prices[4] = 6. Is it a new low (< minPrice 1)?
frames.note: Each day is one of two things: a cheaper BUY (lower the floor) or a SELL candidate against the cheapest buy so far. The single comparison prices[right] < minPrice decides which.

frames.caption: Sell today: 6 − minPrice 1 = 5. best = max(0, 5) = 5. (buy@1 → sell@6)
frames.note: Today is NOT a new low, so it is a candidate SELL day. The best you could do is sell now against the cheapest buy seen so far: prices[right] − minPrice. Keep the running maximum in `best`. This is the entire trick — "min-so-far + running best" — a one-directional window where left snaps to each new minimum and right sweeps forward, never a shrink loop. Because each price is read exactly once, the scan is O(n) time and O(1) space.

frames.caption: right=5: today's price prices[5] = 4. Is it a new low (< minPrice 1)?
frames.note: Each day is one of two things: a cheaper BUY (lower the floor) or a SELL candidate against the cheapest buy so far. The single comparison prices[right] < minPrice decides which.

frames.caption: Sell today: 4 − minPrice 1 = 3. best = max(5, 3) = 5. (buy@1 → sell@4)
frames.note: Today is NOT a new low, so it is a candidate SELL day. The best you could do is sell now against the cheapest buy seen so far: prices[right] − minPrice. Keep the running maximum in `best`. This is the entire trick — "min-so-far + running best" — a one-directional window where left snaps to each new minimum and right sweeps forward, never a shrink loop. Because each price is read exactly once, the scan is O(n) time and O(1) space.

frames.caption: End of prices. The largest gap found is 5 — buy at 1 (index 1), sell at 6 (index 4).
frames.note: Each price entered the scan once and was tested once, so the work is O(n) with O(1) extra state — just `minPrice` and `best`. The answer is the widest profit gap ever recorded, not the final day's profit, which is why best is tracked as a running maximum. Note left (minPrice) never moves backward, mirroring how a window's pointers only advance.
